raw_case:
Concurrent acute appendicitis and obstructive ureterolithiasis: a case report and review of literature


Abstract

The prediction of the coexistence of acute appendicitis and renal colic can be challenging, especially when the patient’s symptoms point toward one diagnosis. In this case report, we describe a patient who presented to the emergency department with severe lower abdominal pain that was thought to be solely due to acute appendicitis. Further evaluation, however, revealed the simultaneous coexistence of a right ureteral stone, causing severe hydroureteronephrosis. The patient underwent prompt surgical management, including laparoscopic appendectomy, ureteroscopy, and double-J stent insertion, and had an uneventful postoperative recovery.

Introduction

Right lower abdominal pain is one of the most common surgical emergencies, with appendicitis being a frequent cause [1]. Renal colic due to a ureteral stone is also a common reason for this complaint. However, the simultaneous occurrence of these two acute pathologies is rare. To the best of our knowledge, only seven cases of acute appendicitis concurrent with a right ureteral stone and one case with a left ureteral stone have been reported in the medical literature [2–9]. Herein, we report a case of a male patient who presented with symptoms suggestive of appendicitis. Subsequent abdominal and pelvic computed tomography (CT) imaging revealed, however, the presence of right-sided ureterolithiasis in addition to acute appendicitis.

Case presentation

A 30-year-old male patient presented to the emergency department with a severe right lower quadrant abdominal pain for the past 12 hours. He also reported experiencing nausea, a loss of appetite, and a single episode of vomiting. There had been no change in bowel or urinary habits since the onset of pain. His past medical history was unremarkable, and he had no prior surgeries or use of medications for chronic diseases. Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented. The abdomen was soft and tender, with guarding and rebound tenderness noted in the right iliac fossa. The patient was afebrile and hemodynamically stable, but the blood pressure was elevated at 200/100 mm Hg. Laboratory workup was significant for leukocytosis (WBC 22.06 × 109/L) with 89.60% neutrophils. The absolute neutrophil count (ANC) was elevated (20 × 109/L). Creatinine was also noted to be at borderline (102 μmol/L). Urine dipstick and urine microscopy were unremarkable. Other laboratory values outside the reference ranges are shown in Table 1.An IV contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed an 8-mm proximal to mid-ureteral obstructive stone on the right side (Fig. 1), causing severe hydronephrosis of the right kidney and proximal hydroureter (Fig. 2).Multiple tiny obstructive stones were also noted bilaterally. The appendix measured approximately 1.4 cm in maximum caliber, with an appendicolith present within the appendiceal lumen (Fig. 3a). Significant surrounding fat stranding was noted around the appendix, and multiple enlarged lymph nodes with signs of reactive inflammation were seen adjacent to the cecum (Fig. 3b).The cardiologist was consulted regarding the patient's elevated blood pressure and recommended pain control. The urologist was also consulted and advised on the observation of creatinine levels. The plan was an admission of the patient with NPO, IV fluids, antibiotics, analgesia, PPI, valsartan 80 mg STAT, and to undergo an urgent laparoscopic appendectomy with right ureteroscopy and double-J stent insertion. The patient underwent the surgery on the same day successfully, and the intraoperative findings revealed an acutely inflamed appendix with the presence of exudates, but no evidence of perforation, abscess formation, or gangrene, and the base appeared healthy. A postoperative KUB was performed the next morning, revealing that the right double-J catheter was appropriately placed. The patient was doing well on the first postoperative day with no complaints and was discharged with a course of ciprofloxacin. Out-patient follow-up appointments were scheduled with the urologist, cardiologist, and general surgeon. The resected appendix specimen was sent for histopathological examination, which later revealed focal mucosal ulcerations with transmural neutrophilic infiltration. The inflammatory process extended through the serosa and into the mesoappendiceal fat with no evidence of atypia or malignancy observed.

Discussion

Our patient presented with appendicitis, with incidental findings of a right ureteral stone causing severe hydroureteronephrosis. The chief complaint in the present case was right lower quadrant abdominal pain, which was suggestive of acute appendicitis. Similar to acute appendicitis, ureterolithiasis also provokes colicky abdominal pain. However, in most cases, when acute appendicitis is diagnosed initially, ureterolithiasis is often overlooked in emergency care. Therefore, the concurrent presence of both these disease conditions has been rarely described in the literature. A literature review revealed only a few similar cases (Table 2). This case highlights the importance of maintaining a high index of suspicion for the coexistence of multiple pathologies in patients with RLQ abdominal pain. While scoring systems like the Alvarado Score can be valuable diagnostic tools, they may not reliably predict the presence of concurrent pathologies. CT scanning has become the gold standard diagnostic modality for evaluating right lower quadrant abdominal pain, with high sensitivity and specificity for acute appendicitis and nephrolithiasis [10–13]. This makes CT the preferred approach when multiple acute pathologies in the right lower abdomen are suspected [14]. The management of patients with concurrent acute appendicitis and obstructive ureterolithiasis typically involves a multidisciplinary approach involving general surgeons and urologists. In most cases, surgical intervention is warranted. Laparoscopic appendectomy is the standard treatment for acute appendicitis, while ureteroscopy with stone extraction and stent placement is the preferred treatment for obstructive ureterolithiasis. In our case, both acute pathologies were managed appropriately through prompt surgical interventions. Our treatment approach is in line with other cases of concurrent disease processes reported in the literature.

Conclusions

The diagnosis of abdominal pain can often be achieved through a thorough clinical examination supported by laboratory tests, sometimes eliminating the need for further imaging studies. However, this approach may not be suitable for evaluating patients with right lower abdominal pain, as it may fail to predict the synchronous presentation of multiple pathologies. This case underscores the need for a comprehensive workup, including appropriate imaging, when evaluating patients with RLQ abdominal pain. Though such presentations are uncommon, clinicians should maintain a high index of suspicion for concurrent conditions.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 30-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint:** Severe right lower quadrant abdominal pain for the past 12 hours.
- **History of Present Illness:** Associated symptoms included nausea, loss of appetite, and a single episode of vomiting. No changes in bowel or urinary habits.
- **Past Medical History:** Unremarkable; no prior surgeries or chronic disease medications.
- **Personal History:** None provided.
- **Family History:** None provided.
- **Physical Examination:** Alert and oriented; abdomen soft but with tenderness, guarding, and rebound tenderness in the right iliac fossa; afebrile; hemodynamically stable; elevated blood pressure (200/100 mm Hg).
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Leukocytosis (WBC 22.06 × 10^9/L with 89.60% neutrophils) and elevated ANC (20 × 10^9/L).
  - Borderline elevated creatinine (102 μmol/L); urine dipstick and microscopy were normal.
  - CT abdomen/pelvis:
    - Acute appendicitis with appendicolith, surrounding fat stranding, and reactive lymphadenopathy.
    - Right-sided 8-mm ureteral stone causing severe hydronephrosis and proximal hydroureter.
    - Bilateral tiny obstructive stones.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Acute Appendicitis:**
   - Considered due to localized right lower quadrant pain, guarding, rebound tenderness, leukocytosis with neutrophilia, and CT findings of appendiceal inflammation with an appendicolith and surrounding fat stranding.
   - Confirmed through intraoperative findings and histopathology, revealing transmural neutrophilic infiltration and acute inflammation of the appendix.

2. **Obstructive Ureterolithiasis (Renal Colic):**
   - Right-sided colicky pain could be consistent with a ureteral stone.
   - Confirmed by CT imaging showing an 8-mm obstructive stone, severe hydronephrosis, and bilateral small calculi.

3. **Other Differential Diagnoses (Excluded):**
   - **Right Ovarian or Gynecologic Pathology:** Excluded due to male sex.
   - **Pelvic Inflammatory Disease or Gastrointestinal Perforation:** No fever, bowel habit changes, or free air/abscess on imaging.
   - **Other renal pathologies (e.g., pyelonephritis):** Normal urine dipstick/microscopy; no symptoms of infection or fever.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Concurrent acute appendicitis and obstructive right ureterolithiasis. This was supported by clinical findings, elevated inflammatory markers, and confirmatory imaging with CT, showing evidence of both pathologies. Prompt surgical interventions—laparoscopic appendectomy and ureteroscopy with stent placement—addressed the dual conditions effectively.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Concurrent acute appendicitis and obstructive ureterolithiasis.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 30-year-old male presenting with severe right lower quadrant abdominal pain, which is a key symptom suggestive of appendicitis.

<step 2> Associated symptoms like nausea, loss of appetite, and vomiting further strengthen the suspicion of appendicitis. The absence of bowel or urinary habit changes is less specific but does not rule out appendicitis, and is important to note for differential diagnosis.

<step 3> The physical examination reveals right iliac fossa tenderness, guarding, and rebound tenderness, which are classic signs of peritoneal irritation commonly seen in appendicitis. The patient is afebrile and hemodynamically stable, which can be seen in early appendicitis, but the significantly elevated blood pressure (200/100 mm Hg) is a noteworthy finding that warrants further consideration and might not be directly explained by typical appendicitis alone.

<step 4> Laboratory tests show leukocytosis (WBC 22.06 × 10^9/L) with neutrophilia (89.60% neutrophils), indicating an acute inflammatory process, highly consistent with appendicitis. Borderline elevated creatinine (102 μmol/L) could be related to dehydration or potentially early renal involvement, which needs to be correlated with imaging findings. Normal urine dipstick and microscopy initially might seem less indicative of a urinary tract problem, but this needs to be interpreted in light of the CT scan results.

<step 5> The CT abdomen/pelvis is crucial. It confirms acute appendicitis with typical features like appendicolith, fat stranding, and reactive lymphadenopathy.  Importantly, it also reveals a right-sided 8-mm ureteral stone causing severe hydronephrosis and proximal hydroureter, along with bilateral tiny obstructive stones. This indicates the presence of both acute appendicitis and a significant urological issue.

<step 6> Integrating all findings, the patient has clinical and radiological evidence of both acute appendicitis and right ureteral stone with hydronephrosis. Both conditions can cause right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and vomiting.  The leukocytosis is consistent with both inflammatory processes. The physical exam findings of RLQ tenderness, guarding, and rebound are highly suggestive of appendicitis. The CT scan definitively confirms both diagnoses. The elevated blood pressure could be a physiological response to severe pain from either condition, or potentially related to the hydronephrosis and its impact on renal function.

<step 7> Considering the clinical picture and CT findings, the final diagnosis should include both conditions as they are concurrently present and contribute to the patient's symptoms and clinical presentation.  While appendicitis is often considered a surgical emergency, the ureteral stone with hydronephrosis is also a significant finding requiring management.

### Answer:
Acute Appendicitis and Right Ureteral Stone with Hydronephrosis

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a 30-year-old male presenting with severe right lower quadrant abdominal pain, which is a key symptom suggestive of appendicitis.

Associated symptoms like nausea, loss of appetite, and vomiting further strengthen the suspicion of appendicitis. The absence of bowel or urinary habit changes is less specific but does not rule out appendicitis, and is important to note for differential diagnosis.

The physical examination reveals right iliac fossa tenderness, guarding, and rebound tenderness, which are classic signs of peritoneal irritation commonly seen in appendicitis. The patient is afebrile and hemodynamically stable, which can be seen in early appendicitis, but the significantly elevated blood pressure (200/100 mm Hg) is a noteworthy finding that warrants further consideration and might not be directly explained by typical appendicitis alone.

Laboratory tests show leukocytosis (WBC 22.06 × 10^9/L) with neutrophilia (89.60% neutrophils), indicating an acute inflammatory process, highly consistent with appendicitis. Borderline elevated creatinine (102 μmol/L) could be related to dehydration or potentially early renal involvement, which needs to be correlated with imaging findings. Normal urine dipstick and microscopy initially might seem less indicative of a urinary tract problem, but this needs to be interpreted in light of the CT scan results.

The CT abdomen/pelvis is crucial. It confirms acute appendicitis with typical features like appendicolith, fat stranding, and reactive lymphadenopathy.  Importantly, it also reveals a right-sided 8-mm ureteral stone causing severe hydronephrosis and proximal hydroureter, along with bilateral tiny obstructive stones. This indicates the presence of both acute appendicitis and a significant urological issue.

Integrating all findings, the patient has clinical and radiological evidence of both acute appendicitis and right ureteral stone with hydronephrosis. Both conditions can cause right lower quadrant pain, nausea, and vomiting.  The leukocytosis is consistent with both inflammatory processes. The physical exam findings of RLQ tenderness, guarding, and rebound are highly suggestive of appendicitis. The CT scan definitively confirms both diagnoses. The elevated blood pressure could be a physiological response to severe pain from either condition, or potentially related to the hydronephrosis and its impact on renal function.

Considering the clinical picture and CT findings, the final diagnosis should include both conditions as they are concurrently present and contribute to the patient's symptoms and clinical presentation.  While appendicitis is often considered a surgical emergency, the ureteral stone with hydronephrosis is also a significant finding requiring management.

<Step 2> Confirmed through intraoperative findings and histopathology, revealing transmural neutrophilic infiltration and acute inflammation of the appendix.
<Step 3> Right-sided colicky pain consistent with a ureteral stone.
<Step 4> Obstructive Ureterolithiasis confirmed by CT imaging showing an 8-mm obstructive stone, severe hydronephrosis, and bilateral small calculi.
<Step 5> Right Ovarian or Gynecologic Pathology excluded due to male sex.
<Step 6> Pelvic Inflammatory Disease or Gastrointestinal Perforation excluded by absence of fever, bowel habit changes, and no free air/abscess on imaging.
<Step 7> Other renal pathologies (e.g., pyelonephritis) excluded by normal urine dipstick/microscopy and absence of symptoms of infection or fever.
<Step 8> Concurrent acute appendicitis and obstructive right ureterolithiasis diagnosed based on clinical findings, elevated inflammatory markers, and confirmatory imaging with CT.
<Step 9> Surgical interventions included laparoscopic appendectomy and ureteroscopy with stent placement.
<Step 10> Dual conditions effectively addressed by the surgical interventions.
